channel.title: Jacob's Research Journal
channel.description: Deep research papers and analysis on AI, technology, and public life — by Jacob Brooke in Jefferson City, MO.

item.title: Google's Gemma 4: What Actually Matters
item.description: Google dropped Gemma 4 on April 2nd with Apache 2.0 licensing — and that legal change may matter more than any benchmark. A deep look at the model family, architecture innovations, Mac setup, and an honest read of where it actually stands vs. Qwen, Llama 4, and the Chinese open-model pack.
item.content:encoded:
Google dropped Gemma 4 on April 2nd, and for once the open-weights release cycle is moving faster than the hype cycle can catch up. I've spent the last couple days working through the technical docs, running the models locally, and reading the community reaction. Here's what I actually think.

The thing that caught my eye about Gemma 4 wasn't a benchmark number. It was a license. Apache 2.0 — genuinely, with no asterisks, no carve-outs. Previous Gemma versions shipped with a custom Terms of Use that included monthly active user caps and acceptable-use restrictions. That pushed commercial teams toward Mistral, Qwen, and Llama instead. Apache 2.0 removes the friction entirely.

The Lineup

Four models, two tiers. The edge tier (E2B at 2.3B effective / 5.1B total, E4B at 4.5B effective / 8.0B total) targets on-device deployment with 128K context. The workstation tier (26B MoE with 4B active parameters, 31B Dense) targets server and desktop with 256K context. The "E" stands for "effective" — Google's shorthand for Per-Layer Embeddings, a technique that gives small models representational depth well beyond their parameter count.

Architecture

Three innovations worth understanding: alternating attention (local sliding-window + global full-context, more efficient than dense full attention throughout), dual RoPE (standard for local layers, Proportional RoPE for global, enabling reliable long-context performance), and built-in multimodal encoders (vision across all models, audio on the edge variants).

Benchmarks

The 31B Dense scores well: MMLU Pro 85.2%, GPQA Diamond 84.3%, Arena ELO ~1452 (#3 among open models). The 26B MoE at only 4B active parameters holds an ELO of ~1441 — impressive efficiency. But the speed problem is real: 11 tokens/sec where Qwen 3.5 does 60+. On aggregate benchmarks, Chinese open models (Qwen 3.5, GLM-5, Kimi K2.5) remain competitive or slightly ahead.

Running on Mac

The E4B quantized to GGUF is ~9.6GB — comfortably fits a Mac mini M4. ollama run gemma4:e4b is the easiest entry point. The 26B MoE at 18GB fits a Mac Studio M3 Ultra.

Bottom Line

The Apache 2.0 license change is the real story. If you avoided Gemma because of the terms: reconsider. If you're evaluating for a new project: test the 26B MoE, but benchmark inference speed on your hardware first. The open-weights frontier in April 2026 is genuinely competitive — that's the actual story here.

Read the full post →
